transcript:
GM GM. I'm Jun from Bulk. We're building a next-gen perps exchange on Solana. A little cool backstory about us: we started out as market makers on Solana, and we market made various venues, spot and perps. Through that, we quickly realized that Solana is the best chain out there to build on, the best chain that also has the highest amount of net inflow of assets, various communities, and various products. But it lacks a serious trading experience. And it all boils down to a couple of things: latency, robust risk engines, and user experience. And we are here to solve that.

Our aim is to provide execution worth quoting into. And it all boils down to blending in performance with decentralization. And we provide out three different things. One's extremely fast and pipelined ticks—so essentially that translates to 20 milliseconds of latencies. And then multi-operator owned matching, where you have multiple validators owning the matching engine, the risk engine, various settlements, and various other parts of the stack that can be run independently by any validator. And then the most important part: a real-time risk engine that actively protects you whenever you're trading perps.

Talking a bit more about what ticks are. Essentially ticks can be thought of as blocks in terms of Bulk. Essentially three things go into ticks. One's data transactions, like oracles and risk signals and various other updates. And cancel and post priority orders. And the bucket number three holds all other market transactions: limit, conditionals, and various other things that you want to put. But we arrange bucket number two and bucket number three transactions in pseudorandom order to prevent last-look attacks and also give priority to market makers and latency-sensitive participants who want to post cancels and then post.

And this also brings us to the next part about how we give this 20 milliseconds of performance into order pipelining. An async pipelined execution that takes place in two phases. Phase one of that is we collect all the orders, send it out to all the validators. All the validators create something called minisketch, which I'll be talking a bit more in the next slides. And then we finalize the minisketch, push it to matching. Don't wait for state confirmation, keep this pipeline moving. But in the next 20 to 40 milliseconds, create a new state digest, finalize that, and commit that into a BFT kind of chain. So keeping this in a very fast pipeline way, we get 0 to 20 milliseconds of latencies for all these sorts of market makers or any other HFT participants. And then 20 to 40 milliseconds of hard finality for people who want hardcore finality on the state.

And talking a bit more about why latency is very, very important in high-frequency trading is that—this is the Monte Carlo distribution of price over frequency. And from our simulations, we found out that every one second you lose, you lose up to one bips in price changes. And it's very, very important for a participant who is very latency focused. And with Solana's 400 block times, you lose up to 0.06 bips on-chain. And compare that to Binance, who is the liquidity leader right now, you always end up losing on your trades. And market makers or any other HFT participants who are quoting, they always make a loss. And there's a bunch of things that go on, like arbitrage and a bunch of other things, which are good and which are also bad. And our goal with Bulk: keep that latency as close to Binance as possible, at 20 milliseconds. So don't wait for risk checks, don't wait for anything, pipeline everything very, very fast and push it to matching in 20 milliseconds.

And moving to how all this happens is Broadcast version 1. Right now, Bulk's testnet, that's taking place in Europe with 5% of Solana's stake, runs on an all-to-all broadcast model. All nodes broadcast to each other, create minisketches, move to execution. And version 2 would be a global view where you can also embed DoubleZero or Alpenglow rails of having this tree-based model or stake-based model, wherever you want to do it. Have different regions with 5 to 20 milliseconds of localized latencies and a way to globally sync them in some way.

And over the—at the last couple of months, we essentially spent a lot of time into this to build up a new sort of consensus. A state-of-the-art consensus that essentially is dual-phased, is leaderless, and also does state and execution separation. And at phase one, all we are trying to do is agree on what orders to use for this execution. And then phase number two is what the result of this execution happened, let's finalize that.

But how do we agree? It's the hardest problem right now that we have. And with leader-based solutions, like Solana's leaders today, we have a couple of adversarial things happening. Could be malicious leaders, censorship, and a bunch of other things that go into that. And then we have single operator based exchanges or, you could call, sequencers. Again, censorship, just a different model than a centralized exchange with different custody setups. So we thought about this a lot more and we came up with something we call set reconciliation with minisketch. I'll talk a little bit more about minisketch in the next slide, but the flow is: every honest node creates a minisketch after sending out the orders. And these minisketches can be computed locally. And if there is no 2f+1, you request for any of the missing 2f+1, and then you proceed to matching.

And what is minisketch? Minisketch is a BCH-based library, first developed in Bitcoin Core. There's been a bunch of research towards how we can do set reconciliation over the last couple of decades. But you can think of minisketch as a way you can—it's a tiny checksum of set IDs. It has a fixed capacity C, but as long as the difference of two sets is under that C, you can easily decode, encode, and do whatever you want with the difference. It's 12 times more bandwidth efficient compared to any other sort of encoding protocols out there. And you could essentially think of it as a way that Bulk encodes all of your orders in these minisketches and sends it out to all the other nodes.

And moving to phase number two, it's all about how we agree what happens after matching. So we create a state digest, we again sign it—use a BLS, use anything that's very fast—and they all need 2f+1 again. And after that, essentially create a state update to Solana, change the state on the program on Solana that's taking place, that these were the fills, these are the balance changes, this took place in 40 milliseconds. And every single round, phase one or phase two, both needs 2f+1 signatures to go ahead.

And this gives us, again, the BFT securities and all these sorts of things. We obviously need 3f+1 operators to be alive. 2f+1 votes for phase one, phase two. And this gives us security up to f faulty Byzantine nodes anytime. And the best part about it is you can keep it very simple. You don't need very advanced ways of keeping it alive. And this is very much inspired by Alpenglow's 20% security model. And the best part about minisketch is that it acts as an inclusion proof where you can detect early that this node is a censoring node or this node is out of sync. And based on that, you can actually build very robust systems like jailing systems, slashing systems, and various other systems. And also it's very helpful for any RPC API providers that, 'Oh, this node is out of sync, I can just skip this node and send it to some other node.'

And moving to what makes this good and how is this good? By separating this state's agreement with execution, we get to keep the latency very, very down low at 20 milliseconds. And that gives us a very nice UX for a user and a very good user experience for HFTs and market makers and a bunch of other people, without having to give up any BFT guarantees that we get. And at the same time, it has this leaderless architecture where every node is an independent operator. As long as the whole network, majority of the network has your orders, you can independently propose and have it executed. And then decoupled timelines. You can decouple everything—decouple execution, risk, matching, and a bunch of other components that go into it—without having to worry about consensus being the hardest part. And over the last couple of years, we have noticed that consensus is the blocker. We need at least two to three round trips to confirm something, to agree on something. By doing this, we separate out the most important part, which is very, very safe for market makers to know, 'Oh wow, my order's been accepted at just 2 milliseconds or 20 milliseconds or 40 milliseconds.' So three different sort of commitments that you can take.

And the last and the best part: Solana validators make more money from it. So I'll talk about this a bit more on the last slide, but this whole components is built down into something we call Bulk-Agave, a new validator client that essentially shares 12.5% of Bulk exchange fees to the validators in a new form of fee mode.

And coming down to the last part is we spent the same amount of time on the risk engine, thinking a bit more what is the blocker right now in current risk engines in the design. And I believe that risk engine is the secret sauce of any exchange. And how it's implemented is very, very important. And any margining system is the hardest part about an exchange to run. So we have a couple of goals. Like, let's prevent insolvency: no account is ever allowed to take any position that can't be covered during any stress. Then preserve the latency: don't blow up this computational time of 20 milliseconds. And make it deterministic so that any and every operator around the world can have the same inputs and can come back to the same risk conclusions that this is what happened with the risk. And again with that, we added two more things: portfolio margin, where you will be able to use different sorts of hedging at a portfolio level, and accept any collateral, any and every collateral from Solana and other ecosystems.

And this brings us to how we do it. We use a Markov-based regime system. Essentially we identify what's happening at the market now, whether the market's trading, volatile, dislocated, sidelined, and we optimize your margin for that. Good hedges get better credit, tail risk gets disincentivized, and the whole system just moves along as the regime changes. So instead of saying Bitcoin on your new order is 5% initial margin all the times, we say, oh, your initial margin, or your margin that's needed to cover this position, is a function of your portfolio and the regime that we are in right now. Not a static number on a config file that we wrote two years ago and gets applied everywhere.

And coming to the last part, tying all this together is Bulk-Agave, which houses all the components that I talked about and more, from the consensus to the net and the whole database that we wrote for this. The biggest thinking here is that it runs as a sidecar network on Solana. So you have your standard Solana client which has the same upstream as Jito-Agave right now, and it is essentially the same without touching any of the Solana components. And then we have the Bulk sidecar, or the Bulk Net as we call it. And that has access to all consensus, broadcast, the database, the external APIs, and a bunch of other signals that we need. This gives us a couple of advantages. We get to secure this sidecar network with Solana stake, which is already live and is economical, and it gives us this huge amounts of billions of billions of economic security for this newly created sidecar network. At the same time, validators get 12.5% of the fees, which trickles down to Solana stakers, and that makes Solana more secure, making Bulk more secure. And on top of that, being embedded so in Solana stack, we get to see Solana state very, very fast. Anytime you're a leader, we get to react to it very, very fast, that there is a way for us to read Solana state.

And this boils down to our next vision of creating unified margin accounts, where you will be able to connect your wallet, have your positions on, let's say, USDC Prime on Kamino or a Loopscale vault on Loopscale or something like that, and you'll be able to trade perps with just signing a single message. Don't have to tokenize, don't have to move, don't have to rebalance. All you got to do is connect your wallet, start trading perps, and the risk engine handles all of this. As we have the access to the state, we look at the state, we rebalance you very, very fast, and we keep you safe, keeping Bulk safe along the way. And that essentially makes Bulk an F1 circuit in Solana's highways, where we have Solana specialized for a lot of things, now Bulk is specializing trading for Solana.

Current state of development: we are live on a local cluster, but in the next couple of weeks, our genesis testnet is going to be alive. And anyone will be able to participate in the testnet, trade up to 50x leverage, three different markets, portfolio margin, get to experience the risk engines and all these sorts of things. At the same time, we also have the Bulk-Agave code that is undergoing a bunch of audits and a bunch of other things right now, which will be available to everyone. Right now, 5% of Solana's stake is ready and already running Bulk-Agave, which you can check out from Solana's gossip table right now. And we have a bunch of resources I can share too. But essentially, we are trying to build a new gen perps exchange that doesn't sacrifice on ethos of decentralization but also upholds a lot of performance that we need to battle trading on Solana. Thank you.

sources.title: Stress-Tested and Validated: How Fireblocks and Solana Handled Crypto’s Largest Liquidation Event
sources.snippet:
Crypto's Largest Liquidation Event: Fireblocks & Solana’s Reliability
Fireblocks acquires Dynamic to accelerate onchain adoption for Fintechs and Enterprises.**[Learn more](https://www.fireblocks.com/blog/fireblocks-acquires-dynamic)**
[
![Fireblocks](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/fireblocks-logo.svg)
](https://www.fireblocks.com/)
[Talk to sales](#request-demo)
[Sign in](https://console.fireblocks.io/)
[
![Fireblocks](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/fireblocks-logo.svg)
](https://www.fireblocks.com/)
[Login](https://console.fireblocks.io/)
November 20, 2025
[Industry Insights](https://www.fireblocks.com/category/industry-insights),[Product & Platform Updates](https://www.fireblocks.com/category/product-platform-updates)
# Stress-Tested and Validated: How Fireblocks and Solana Handled Crypto’s Largest Liquidation Event
[![Fireblocks Icon](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/fireblocks-logo.svg)
Avi Bashan
Head of R&D, Wallet Solutions
](https://www.fireblocks.com/author/avi-bashan)
4 min. read
![](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/Solana_Liquidation_Blog-1024x576.webp)
On October 10, 2025,[crypto markets experienced their largest liquidation](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/why-october-2025-down-most-150216105.html)event in history. A whopping $19.5 billion was liquidated across all markets with approximately $1 trillion in total market cap wiped out. Binance halted trading. Ethereum Layer 2s lagged.[Arbitrum fees spiked above $500, with median fees jumping to $116](https://dune.com/ilan_g/oct-10-metrics). The entire ecosystem was under unprecedented stress.
This was the ultimate real-world test of mission-critical infrastructure.
Less than a year after we dramatically improved[Fireblocks’ Solana performance during the Trump meme coin surge](https://www.fireblocks.com/blog/fireblocks-solana-network-performance-optimization), the October 10th liquidation event provided validation of everything we built. Not only did our infrastructure hold, but it excelled, proving that enterprise-grade blockchain operations can handle maximum stress when it matters most.
## When Markets Panic, Infrastructure Must Stay Calm
During the liquidation cascade, Solana processed the chaos without breaking stride. Validators processed[100,000 transactions per second (TPS) of inbound traffic (6x normal peak traffic) while maintaining 400-millisecond block times](https://dune.com/solana_team/october-10-performance-comparison). The network sustained 6,000 raw TPS throughout the event with validators processing transactions with zero payment interruptions.
For Fireblocks customers, the platform handled a 200% increase in Solana traffic with zero customer impact and 100% first-attempt transaction success. This is proof that corporate payments can run on Solana even when markets are in crisis.
## The Numbers That Matter: Digital Asset Infrastructure Performance Under Pressure
Let’s look at what happened to the metrics that matter most during stress events:
* **Transaction Landing Rate: 100%**– No change from baseline. Every transaction succeeded on the first attempt, with no retries needed despite the massive surge in network activity.
* **Broadcasting Speed: 23ms (P50)**– Fireblocks’ median time to broadcast a signed transaction remained unchanged at 23 milliseconds, even as transaction volume spiked 2x. This broadcasting speed also represents a significant improvement from the previous median time of 47ms in March 2025.
* **Time to notify about a completed transaction: 2 seconds**– Consistent throughout the event. Fireblocks’ ability to process and notify about confirmed transactions not only maintained baseline performance during the event, but also showed a 3-second improvement since March 2025.### Transaction Fees: The Real Story
Here’s where Solana’s architecture truly shines. While there was fee variability, median transact

sources.title: How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality | BAM
sources.snippet:
[Back to Blog](https://bam.dev/blog/)

November 12, 2025 • 8 minute read

# How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality

Lucas Bruder

Jito Labs

Share

- _Solana's long-term success depends on deterministic execution infrastructure that scales transparently as the network grows. BAM exists to ensure that as Solana scales, the execution layer remains transparent, fair, and aligned with the applications and users who drive network growth._
- _BAM provides cryptographically verifiable transparency for sequencing that no other solution can provide: every ordering decision proven, not promised._
- _BAM will help everyone using or building on Solana succeed:_
 - _Applications gain the execution certainty needed to offer tighter spreads and predictable fills through Application Controlled Execution (ACE)._
 - _Traders benefit from superior execution quality that rivals or exceeds other venues._
 - _Validators get open-source infrastructure they can verify. When applications succeed on Solana, validators succeed._

# What Is BAM?

Solana stands alone: the only blockchain where trading SOL is cheaper and faster on-chain than on centralized exchanges. With BAM, that lead widens as apps gain the tools to outprice and out-execute other exchanges. BAM provides the execution infrastructure necessary to make on-chain trading not just competitive with centralized venues, but categorically superior.

The Block Assembly Marketplace (BAM), which Jito Labs revealed in July, represents the evolution toward a more transparent and application-driven transaction processing paradigm. BAM addresses a fundamental challenge that has constrained the network's growth: the inability for applications to provide predictable, high-quality execution guarantees to their users.

# Why Solana's Block Building Needed to Evolve

We’ve detected at least seven different transaction schedulers operating on Solana, each with distinct ordering logic and timing characteristics. This fragmentation creates fundamental uncertainty for applications and traders who depend on predictable execution. Traders cannot determine whether they're receiving fair execution or being systematically disadvantaged by opaque scheduling decisions made by validators with competing interests. The result is wider spreads and reduced liquidity as market makers price in execution uncertainty, while developers face inconsistent environments that undermine their ability to compete with centralized alternatives.

# Why Jito Labs Is Building BAM

Jito Labs helped pioneer trading infrastructure on Solana through our Block Engine. While these systems created significant value for validators and improved transaction execution, we've also watched how they've evolved. We've seen concentration dynamics emerge, observed the pressure toward private orderflow relationships, and recognized that without intervention, Solana risks following the same path toward opacity that degraded Ethereum's execution layer.

Ethereum's experience with proposer-builder separation (PBS) offers particularly instructive lessons. While PBS introduced competitive block building, it created perverse incentives: builders maximize MEV extraction to win block auctions, often at users' expense. This dynamic leaves the network stuck in a positive feedback loop that can be hard to unwind. Increased sandwich attacks and other forms of extractive MEV pushed orderflow private, as users sought protection through direct builder relationships. Builders offered rebates for exclusive access to this orderflow, and those with the most private flow could pay higher auction fees, winning more blocks and attracting even more exclusive deals. The result is a concentration of power among a small number of builders, increased opacity in transaction processing, and timing games that degraded network performance. Most concerningly, some dominant builders were operated by trading firms with direct financial incentives to exploit

tags.impact_reason: A new perps exchange focusing on low latency is a solid update for the Solana DeFi ecosystem, but its impact will depend on adoption and liquidity.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a custom 'sidecar' module physically bolted onto a Solana validator server rack as the Semantic Anchor. This directly visualizes the core technical innovation of a 'validator-embedded exchange' described in the text. The sidecar represents 'Bulk's' high-speed execution engine, while the main tower represents the Solana infrastructure it enhances. For the text, I chose '20MS'—the key performance metric that quantifies the story's entire premise of rivaling Binance's latency. Making it a holographic system readout from the sidecar integrates it diegetically into the high-tech world.
visual_idea.description: A sleek, monolithic server rack representing a Solana validator stands in a dimly lit, cavernous data center. Physically fused to its side is a compact, high-performance 'sidecar' module made of matte carbon fiber and glowing heat sinks. Crystalline data conduits filled with racing streaks of cyan light connect the two units. From a small projector on the sidecar, a crisp, stable holographic display floats in the air, showing the glowing cyan text '20MS'. The background shows rows of other server racks receding into a deep, out-of-focus background, creating a sense of scale and depth.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A sleek, monolithic server rack, representing a Solana validator, stands monumentally in a cavernous, dimly lit data center, bathed in high-contrast cyber-noir lighting with deep shadows and volumetric light rays. Physically fused to its anodized aluminum side is a compact, high-performance 'sidecar' module, crafted from matte carbon fiber, featuring glowing heat sinks emitting a soft, internal hum. Crystalline data conduits, filled with racing streaks of vibrant cyan light, elegantly connect the two units, reflecting off the polished surfaces. From a small projector on the sidecar, a crisp, stable holographic display floats in the air, prominently showing the glowing cyan text '20MS', shimmering with data fluctuations. The background recedes into an epic, out-of-focus expanse of countless other server racks, creating an overwhelming sense of scale and depth within the vast server farm. Composed for a vertical 3:4 aspect ratio, with the server rack occupying the lower-mid frame, allowing for expansive negative space at the top to accentuate its towering presence and monumental feel. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic composition, volumetric lighting, high contrast, detailed textures, reflective surfaces.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a custom 'sidecar' module physically bolted onto a Solana validator server rack as the Semantic Anchor. This directly visualizes the core technical innovation of a 'validator-embedded exchange' described in the text. The sidecar represents 'Bulk's' high-speed execution engine, while the main tower represents the Solana infrastructure it enhances. For the text, I chose '20MS'—the key performance metric that quantifies the story's entire premise of rivaling Binance's latency. Making it a holographic system readout from the sidecar integrates it diegetically into the high-tech world.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on the physical interface between the anodized aluminum chassis of the Solana validator and the bolted-on carbon fiber 'sidecar' module. Intricate, glowing fiber-optic cables plug into a complex port array, with light pulsing through them at an incredible speed. The focus is on the seamless but distinct engineering, the contrast of materials, and the raw power of the connection. No text is visible in this close-up.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro shot focusing on the physical interface between the sleek, brushed anodized aluminum chassis of the Solana validator and the robust, textured carbon fiber 'sidecar' module, precisely bolted together. Intricate, hyper-detailed glowing fiber-optic cables, reminiscent of a holographic datastream, plug into a complex, tightly packed port array. Vibrant light pulses through the cables at an incredible speed, casting dynamic neon reflections on the reflective surfaces. The composition emphasizes the seamless yet distinct engineering, showcasing the stark contrast of the cool anodized aluminum and the dark, woven carbon fiber, highlighting the raw, electric power of the connection. The scene is bathed in high-contrast cyberpunk lighting, featuring deep shadows and volumetric light that accentuates every intricate detail and texture, creating a cyber-noir atmosphere. Extremely tight macro framing, centrally focused and symmetrical, fills the entire 1:1 square aspect ratio frame with overwhelming detail and texture. No text is visible in this close-up. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, studio render, incredibly sharp focus, intricate detail, high fidelity, cinematic composition.

editorial.headline: Bulk Unveils Validator-Embedded Exchange to Rival Binance Latency
editorial.standfirst: By moving execution into a custom validator sidecar, Bulk achieves 20ms latency and 40ms finality, effectively turning Solana into a high-frequency trading venue.
key_takeaways: 20ms Latency: Achieved via 'Bulk-Agave' sidecar client, bypassing standard block times.
Validator Economics: Operators running the client earn 12.5% of exchange fees; 5% of stake is already live.
Novel Consensus: Utilizes Bitcoin's 'Minisketch' for bandwidth-efficient, leaderless set reconciliation.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The narrative that on-chain trading cannot compete with centralized exchange (CEX) performance is being dismantled at the infrastructure level. **Bulk**, led by CTO Junaid Peer, has revealed its architecture for a derivatives exchange that doesn't just build *on* Solana but integrates *into* it. By forking the Jito-Agave client to create **Bulk-Agave**, the project introduces a validator sidecar network that delivers 20ms matching times—speeds previously exclusive to venues like Binance. This move shifts the battlefield from simple application logic to deep infrastructure optimization, targeting the high-frequency traders (HFTs) who have historically been priced out by Solana's 400ms block times.

The technical differentiator lies in Bulk's adoption of **Minisketch**, a set reconciliation library originally developed for Bitcoin Core. Rather than relying on a single leader—which introduces censorship risks and latency bottlenecks—Bulk employs a leaderless, dual-phase consensus model. Nodes broadcast 'minisketches' (tiny checksums of order sets) to reconcile differences efficiently, consuming 12x less bandwidth than traditional encoding. This allows for an all-to-all broadcast model that achieves soft confirmation in under 20ms and hard finality in 40ms, effectively decoupling execution speed from Solana's base layer consensus while remaining anchored to it.

Critically, Bulk has solved the 'cold start' problem of infrastructure adoption through direct economic alignment. The protocol offers a 12.5% share of exchange fees to validators who run the Bulk-Agave client. This incentive structure has already secured 5% of Solana's stake on the network's testnet. By treating validators as active partners rather than passive securities, Bulk is building a specialized 'F1 circuit' for trading within the broader Solana highway, ensuring that the hardware powering the network has a vested interest in the exchange's liquidity and performance.

Beyond raw speed, the platform introduces a Markov-based regime risk engine that adjusts margin requirements in real-time based on market volatility. This is coupled with a 'Unified Margin' experience, allowing capital parked in protocols like **Kamino** or **Loopscale** to be used as collateral without fragmentation. For institutional investors, this represents the holy grail of capital efficiency: the security and composability of DeFi combined with the execution guarantees of a top-tier CEX.
